claim.statement: The COVID-19 pandemic and its associated control measures have significantly disrupted daily activities, leading to high levels of psychological distress among university students in Jordan and highlighting the need for nationwide psychological support programmes targeted at this population.
claim.statement_location: Conclusion section of the paper (pages near the end, summary of findings).

data.source: Internet-based self-administered questionnaire distributed via social-media platforms (Facebook and WhatsApp).
data.notes: Non-random convenience sampling; online survey format.

method.description: The authors conducted an online cross-sectional survey of university students in Jordan during the national COVID-19 lockdown. Eligible participants (≥18 years, enrolled in Jordanian universities) were invited through social media to complete a questionnaire that captured sociodemographic characteristics, the 10-item Kessler Psychological Distress Scale (K10), coping activities, and major concerns. Data were analysed with SPSS 23 using descriptive statistics, non-parametric tests, correlations and multinomial logistic regression to examine associations between distress levels and selected predictors.
steps: Design online questionnaire containing sociodemographic items, K10 scale, coping and concern questions.
Distribute survey link on Facebook and WhatsApp groups targeting Jordanian university students.
Collect responses and exclude incomplete submissions.
Compute K10 total scores and classify distress severity (none, mild, moderate, severe).
Perform descriptive analyses of sample characteristics and distress levels.
Compare K10 scores by gender using Wilcoxon Rank-Sum test.
Calculate Spearman correlation between age and K10 score.
Run multinomial logistic regression to assess predictors (e.g., motivation for distance learning) of distress severity categories.
method.models: Descriptive statistics; Wilcoxon Rank-Sum test; Spearman rank correlation; Multinomial logistic regression
method.outcome_variable: Psychological distress level (K10 total score and categorical severity)
method.independent_variables: Age, gender, motivation for distance learning, coping activities, smoking status, marital status, study level
method.control_variables: not stated (regression model covariates beyond those listed not specified)

results.summary: Among 381 university students (mean age 22.6 years, 52.2 % female), the mean K10 score was 34.2, with 69.5 % categorised as having severe psychological distress. Females exhibited higher odds of mild and severe distress compared with males. Younger age correlated inversely with distress scores. Lack of motivation for distance learning was strongly associated with severe distress. The most common coping strategy was spending more time on social media, and over half of students identified online distance learning as their top concern during the pandemic.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Odds ratio for severe distress vs high motivation for distance learning
Odds ratio (female vs male) for mild psychological distress
Odds ratio (female vs male) for severe psychological distress

metadata.original_paper_title: Is it Just About Physical Health? An Internet-Based Cross-Sectional Study Exploring the Psychological Impacts of COVID-19 Pandemic on University Students in Jordan Using Kessler Psychological Distress Scale
